Ptarmigan

[NO_PTAR_001]

Author
Affiliation

Anders Kolstad

The Norwegian Institute for Nature Research (NINA)

Published

April 16, 2026

Recommended citation: Kolstad, A. 2026. Ptarmigan [Fjellrype] (ID(s): NO_PTAR_001) v. 1.0.0. ecRxiv: https://github.com/NINAnor/ecRxiv/tree/main/indicators/NO_PTAR_001

Metadata
Log
  • 16 Apr. 2026 - Original PR

1. Summary

  1. Background and rationale: Rock ptarmigan (Lagopus muta) is a herbivourous alpine bird that is found throughout the Norwegian mountain range where it lives year round. It responds to anthropogenic disturbance, including climate change, through their effects on both survival and reporoduction. We also have a relatively good amount of data and knowledge about this species and the different populations. This combines to make ptarmigan a suitable indicator for ecosystem condition in Norwegian mountains. The species is also a popular game bird.

  2. Methods: The ptarmigan population size is estimated from a statistical model, using hunting statistics as the main data source, and with the assumption that 10% of the population is shot each year. This value is then normalised based on a reference level, which is an expert-assessed population estimate based on known breeding density and the amount of suitable habitat. The indicator is developed in the work with the Norwegian Nature Index (Nater and Eriksen 2025). The methods are are described in full on the Nature index web site.

  3. Interpretation: An indicator value of 1 means that the ptamigan is as numerous, or more, than what we expect under the reference condition. Values decreasing toward zero indicates that the average county-level populaton size is decreasing.

  4. Results and development needs: The indicator value for 2024 for Norway as a whole is 0.31 (95% CI = 0.0 - 1), which indicates a relatively severe deviation from the reference condition. The development over time is showing a negative trend. The errors are extremely wide, representing large model uncertainties. The 50% CI is 0.12 - 0.58.

1.2 Native language summary - Norwegian

  1. Bakgrunn og begrunnelse: Rype (Lagopus muta) er en hønsefugl som finnes over hele den norske fjellkjeden, hvor den lever hele året. Arten er følsom på menneskeskapte forstyrrelser, inkludert klimaendringer, gjennom deres effekter på både overlevelse og reproduksjon. Vi har relativt gode mengder data og kunnskap om denne arten og de ulike bestandene. Dette gjør rypa til en egnet indikator for økologisk tilstand i norske fjellområder. Arten er også en populær jaktfugl og reguleres delvsis gjennom kvotesettingen.

  2. Metoder: Bestandsstørrelsen av rype estimeres ved hjelp av en statistisk modell, der jaktstatistikk er hoveddatakilden. Det brukes en antakelse om at 10 % av bestanden skytes hvert år. Denne verdien normaliseres deretter basert på en referanseverdi, som er et ekspertvurdert bestandsestimat basert på kjent hekketetthet og mengden egnet habitat. Indikatoren er utviklet i arbeidet med Naturindeksen (Nater and Eriksen 2025). Metodene er beskrevet i detalj på Naturindeksens nettsider.

  3. Tolkning: En indikatorverdi på 1 betyr at rypebestanden er like stor eller større enn det vi forventer under referansetilstanden. Verdier som nærmer seg null indikerer at den gjennomsnittlige bestandsstørrelsen på fylkesnivå er avtagende.

  4. Resultater og utviklingsbehov: Indikatorverdien for 2024 for Norge er 0,35 (95 % KI = 0,15–0,52), noe som indikerer et relativt stort avvik fra referansetilstanden. Utviklingen over tid viser en negativ trend.

2. About the underlying data

The data comes from hunting statistics (harvest bag data) published by SSB (Statistics Norway). The procedure to download the data is described here.

2.1 Spatial and temporal resolution and extent

Indicator values are available for the different assessment years in Naturindeksen: 1990, 2020, 2010, 2011, 2012, 2013, 2014, 2019 and 2024. To produce indicator estimates for these years, one uses the average over 2-3 year. For the year 2024, data from two hunting seasons were included (2022/23-2023/24).

The indicator is calculated for all counties in Norway. Here we report the values for five geographic regions.

2.2 Sampling design

The data originates from hunters when they report their catches, something they are required to do. The sampling/hunting effort is not known, and assumed to be stable. This is a rather big assumption.

2.3 Original units

The original units of the raw data is the number of individuals. The data are assigned to spatial units, so this could be turned into measures of density.

2.4 Instructions for citing, using and accessing data

The underlying data (hunting statistics) are available via SSB, but not in a prepared format (see here).

Indicator point estimates and quantiles, for different geographic units, are freely available (Nater et al. 2025).

3. Indicator properties

3.1 Ecosystem Condition Typology Class (ECT)

B1 - Compositional state characteristics

3.2 Ecosystem condition characteristic

The ecosystem characteristic represented in this indicator is an ecosystem where species abundances and composition is similar to that in the reference condition.

3.3 Collinearities with other indicators

The indicator might be correlated to other indicators, especially other indicators within ECT class B1, such as the species that prey on ptarmigan (arctic fox, golden eagle), or other species that have similar habitats or way of live (such as willow grouse).

3.4 Impact factors

Ptarmigan is sensitive to several antropogenic pressures, especially climate change and hunting, but also habitat destruction and disturbance by human activities.

3.5 Spatial aggregation

The hunting statistics are aggregated to county level using a population model. There the variable value is normalised by scaling and truncating against X100 and X0. The indicator values are then resampled to five geographic regions, and then to Norway, with a sampling weight proportional to the mountain area in each region or area.

4. Reference condition and levels

4.1 Reference condition

The reference condition is a moutain ecosystem with minimal human impact and a climate similar to the period 1961-1990 (Nybø and Evju (red) 2017).

4.2 Reference levels

X100 is defined based on the predicted number of ptarmigan individuals based on a statistical model. This value varies between counties.

X0 is defined as a complete absence of ptarmigan.

X60 is not specified and defaults to 60% of the X100 value.

4.2.1 Spatial resolution and validity

The reference values vary with counties, and normalisation of variable values should be done after aggregating those first to the same county scale.

5. Uncertainties

5.1 General uncertainties

There are obvious limitations to using havest bag data, rather than more standardised monitoring data. This is elaborated in the indicator documentation on Naturindeksen. However, we feel the hunting data still has considerable value and that it is indeed suitable for this purpose of estimating population sizes.

5.2 Reported uncertainties

The reported uncertainties reflect both the uncertainty in the reference levels (something which is accommodated in the naturindeks workflow), the uncertainty in the county estimates, and when spatially aggregating, also the spatial variation between counties.

6. References

Nater, C. R., and L. F. Eriksen. 2025. Naturindeks for Norge 2025. Tilstand og utvikling for biologisk mangfold. {NINA} {Temahefte} 97. Trondheim: Norsk Institutt for Naturforskning.
Nater, C. R., T. Zeiner-Henriksen, and S. Nybø. 2025. NI2025: Indicator Data. doi:10.21345/y790-v762.
Nybø, S., and M. Evju (red). 2017. Fagsystem for fastsetting av god økologisk tilstand - forslag fra et ekspertråd.

7. Import and prepare datasets

7.1 Naturindeks

We download the dataset from the Naturindeks database using the API.

Code 
# password is hidden for now, but the plan is to set up a read only guest access. Therefore I upload teh database export to github for now to ensure reproducibility.
source(here::here("R", "_pw.R"))

# Import data set
ptarImport <- NIcalc::importDatasetApi(
  username = myUser,
  password = myPwd,
  eco = NULL,
  indic = c("Fjellrype"),
  year = c("2010", "2014", "2019", "2024")
)
saveRDS(ptarImport, here::here("data/ptarImport.rds"))

8. Spatial units

Accorinf to the Naturindeks terminology, the Norwegian counties are BSunits, the 15 counties are IC units, and the five geographic regions in Norway (east, south, west, central, north) are NI units. We can think of the counties as ecosystem assets, and mainland Norway as the Ecosystem accounting Area.

9. Analyses

Here I use a generic approach to handling dta from the Naturindeks where I get both the variable values and the reference values, separately, from the NI database, and perform the normalisation and spatial aggregation ourselves.

The reference values have uncertainties attached to them, which we will ignore here. The variable values have uncertainties as well, in the form of probability function. The uncertainties are from a statistical model, and so we think of them as inferrential uncertainties.

Here I use an NIcalc function to just prepare and structure the data a little differently.

Code 
# Assemble
ptarAssemble <- NIcalc::assembleNiObject(
  inputData = ptarImport,
  predefNIunits = c(allArea = T, parts = T, counties = F),
  indexType = "thematic"
)

Check input data ..... 

Elicitate incomplete reference values and indicator observations ..... 

Assemble dataset .....

Then I get the reference values. These are unique to each county (ICunit). I only have an X100 value. X0 is a natural zero.

Code 
x100 <- ptarImport$referenceValues$referenceValues |>
  select(ICunitId, ICunitName, x100 = expectedValue)
x100
# A tibble: 15 × 3
   ICunitId ICunitName        x100
      <int> <chr>            <dbl>
 1     1391 Hedmark           49.3
 2     1392 Oppland          114. 
 3     1393 Buskerud          42.3
 4     1394 Telemark          38.6
 5     1395 Aust-Agder        19.7
 6     1396 Vest-Agder        17.7
 7     1397 Rogaland          37.6
 8     1398 Hordaland         97.5
 9     1399 Sogn og Fjordane 116. 
10     1400 Møre og Romsdal   72.8
11     1401 Sør-Trøndelag     62.0
12     1402 Nord-Trøndelag    63.0
13     1403 Nordland         213. 
14     1404 Troms            178. 
15     1405 Finnmark         299.

The variable values are stored as a parameterised probability function, and I need to draw n samples from this function. The probability function differs between indicators in the Naturindeks. It could also differ within an indicator. Here I create a vector defining which probability function to use. I use ´tradObs´ where there is a regular probability distribution family, and customObs where customDist!= NA. When distributionFamilyName == NA, customDistribution != NA.

Code 
myYears <- as.character(c(2010, 2014, 2019, 2024))
obstype <- NULL

for (i in 1:length(myYears)) {
  obs <- ptarAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$distributionFamilyName

  obs[!is.na(obs)] <- "tradObs"
  obs[is.na(obs)] <- "customObs"

  obstype[[i]] <- obs
}

Now I am ready to sample the probability distributions. I set n to be 1000.

Code 
dat <- tibble(ICunitId = NULL, value = NULL, year = NULL)

for (i in 1:length(myYears)) {
  #print(i)
  myMat <- NIcalc::sampleObsMat(
    ICunitId = ptarAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$ICunitId,
    value = ptarAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$expectedValue,
    distrib = ptarAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$distributionFamilyName,
    mu = ptarAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$distParameter1,
    sig = ptarAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$distParameter2,
    customDistribution = ptarAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$customDistribution,
    obsType = obstype[[i]],
    nsim = 1000
  )
  ICunitIds <- row.names(myMat)
  
  myMat <-  myMat |>
    as_tibble() |>
    add_column(ICunitId = ICunitIds, .before = 1) |>
    pivot_longer(cols = -any_of("ICunitId")) |>
    select(-name) |>
    add_column(year = myYears[i])
  
  dat <- dat |> 
    bind_rows(myMat)
}
head(dat)
# A tibble: 6 × 3
  ICunitId value year 
  <chr>    <dbl> <chr>
1 1391     45.8  2010 
2 1391     34.6  2010 
3 1391     22.0  2010 
4 1391      9.78 2010 
5 1391     41.5  2010 
6 1391     12.2  2010

This is the point in the spatial aggregation where we should normalise against the reference values. So let’s add inn the X100 values.

Code 
dat <- dat |>
  left_join(x100 |> mutate(ICunitId = as.character(ICunitId)), by = join_by(ICunitId))

Then we can normalise also the scatter plot with variables against indicator values (Figure 1).

Code 
ind <- ec_normalise(
  variable = dat$value,
  x100 = dat$x100
)

dat <- dat |> add_column(indicator = ind)
Code 
ec_norm_plot(dat$value, dat$indicator) +
  facet_wrap(.~dat$ICunitName) 
ggsave(here::here("img/norm_plot_eng.png"), scale =1.5)
Code 
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("img/norm_plot_eng.png"))

I also export a figure for the Norwegian fact sheet:

Export normalisation function plots 
ec_norm_plot(dat$value, dat$indicator, lan = "norsk") +
  facet_wrap(.~dat$ICunitName)
ggsave(here::here("img/norm_plot_facet.png"), scale =1.5)

ec_norm_plot(dat$value, dat$indicator, lan = "norsk")
ggsave(here::here("img/norm_plot.png"), scale =1)

Then we can aggregate by a weighted resampling from counties to geographic regions, and then to Norway. For this we need to know which geographic region (N, M, E, S, W) the ICunits belong to.

Code 
# In this object we get the rows per municipality (BSunits), with mountain area ("Fjell") and geographic region ("part").  
aux <- as_tibble(ptarAssemble$BSunits) |>
  rename(BSunitId = id)
head(aux)
# A tibble: 6 × 10
  BSunitId Fjell name        totalArea terrestrialArea marineArea county  part 
     <int> <dbl> <chr>           <dbl>           <dbl>      <dbl> <chr>   <chr>
1      428  255. Trysil          3017.           3017.          0 Hedmark E    
2      430  386. Stor-Elvdal     2171.           2171.          0 Hedmark E    
3      432  800. Rendalen        3180.           3180.          0 Hedmark E    
4      434  550. Engerdal        2198.           2198.          0 Hedmark E    
5      436  462. Tolga           1124.           1124.          0 Hedmark E    
6      437  644. Tynset          1880.           1880.          0 Hedmark E    
# ℹ 2 more variables: centerLat <dbl>, centerLong <dbl>

The data is at the ICunit level (counties) so wee need to add the ICunitId into here.

Code 
IC_BC_link <- ptarImport$ICunits |>
  select(ICunitId = id, BSunitId)

aux <- aux |>
  left_join(IC_BC_link, by = join_by(BSunitId))

Then we can sum the mountain area, and move that value, and the geographic region, into the main dataset. We use the alpine area that is recorded in the Naturindeks database. They should represenet the area above the empirical forest line.

Code 
aux <- aux |>
  group_by(ICunitId) |>
  summarise(alpine_area = sum(Fjell), part = unique(part)) |>
  mutate(ICunitId = as.character(ICunitId))

dat <- dat |>
  left_join(aux, by = join_by(ICunitId))

Then we do a weighted resampling from counties to geographic regions.

Code 
regions <- c("N", "C", "W", "S", "E")


out <- tibble(id = seq(1,1000))

for (i in regions) {
  temp_reg <- dat |>
    filter(part == i)
  for (n in myYears) {
    temp_year <- temp_reg |>
      filter(year == n)

    col <- sample(
      x = temp_year$indicator,
      prob = temp_year$alpine_area,
      size = 1000,
      replace = TRUE
    )

    name <- paste(i, n, sep = "_")

    out <- out |>
      add_column(!!name := col)
  }
}
head(out)
# A tibble: 6 × 21
     id N_2010 N_2014 N_2019 N_2024 C_2010 C_2014 C_2019 C_2024 W_2010 W_2014
  <int>  <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>
1     1 0.163   0.623 0.613   0.655  1      0.555  0.799  0.442  0.379 0.397 
2     2 0.377   0.370 0.868   0.623  0.782  0.591  0.860  0.343  0.209 0.0148
3     3 0.206   0.939 0.0317  0.439  0.148  0.636  0.495  0.485  0.347 0.0947
4     4 0.0540  0.912 0.703   0.894  0.350  1      0.850  0.269  0.496 0.144 
5     5 0.425   0.121 0.0683  0.327  0.616  0.305  0.588  0.165  0.437 0.185 
6     6 0.375   0.101 1       0.510  0.330  0.690  0.454  0.973  0.342 0.169 
# ℹ 10 more variables: W_2019 <dbl>, W_2024 <dbl>, S_2010 <dbl>, S_2014 <dbl>,
#   S_2019 <dbl>, S_2024 <dbl>, E_2010 <dbl>, E_2014 <dbl>, E_2019 <dbl>,
#   E_2024 <dbl>

To get the national indicator distribution, we will do a weighted resampling from geographic regions to national scale. So we need the summed mountain area per geographic region.

Code 
aux2 <- aux |>
  group_by(part) |>
  summarise(alpine_area = sum(alpine_area))
aux2
# A tibble: 5 × 2
  part  alpine_area
  <chr>       <dbl>
1 C          16077.
2 E          17373.
3 N          56122.
4 S           6368.
5 W          18503.
Code 
national <- out |>
  pivot_longer(cols = !any_of("id")) |>
  separate_wider_delim(cols = name, delim = "_", names = c("part", "year")) |>
  left_join(aux2, by = join_by(part))

out2 <- tibble(id = seq(1,1000))

for (n in myYears) {
    temp_year <- national |>
      filter(year == n)

    col <- sample(
      x = temp_year$value,
      prob = temp_year$alpine_area,
      size = 1000,
      replace = TRUE
    )

    name <- paste("All", n, sep = "_")

    out2 <- out2 |>
      add_column(!!name := col)
}

head(out2)
# A tibble: 6 × 5
     id All_2010 All_2014 All_2019 All_2024
  <int>    <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>
1     1   0.335     0.902    0.171   0.119 
2     2   0.0775    0.824    0.168   0.204 
3     3   0.338     0.190    0.615   0.0219
4     4   0.804     0.117    0.427   0.0723
5     5   1         0.524    0.140   0.608 
6     6   0.987     0.537    0.259   0.501

Then we append this to the main dataset

Code 
out <- out |>
  cbind(out2 |> select(-id))

10. Results

Then we create some summary statistics (Table 2) and plot them (Figure 2).

Code 
summary_out <- out |>
  pivot_longer(cols = !any_of("id")) |>
  separate_wider_delim(cols = name, delim = "_", names = c("part", "year")) |>
  group_by(part, year) |>
  summarise(
    median = round(median(value), 2),
    p_025 = round(quantile(value, probs = 0.0025), 2),
    p_25 = round(quantile(value, probs = 0.25), 2),
    p_75 = round(quantile(value, probs = 0.75), 2),
    p_975 = round(quantile(value, probs = 0.975), 2),
  ) |>
  add_column(
    ECT = "B1",
    Indicator = "Ptarmigan",
    Ecosystem = "Mountains",
    ID = "NO_PTAR_001"
  )
Code 
summary_out |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = median, colour = part, group = part)) +
  geom_pointrange(
    aes(ymin = p_025, ymax = p_975),
    size = 2,
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.3)
  ) +
  geom_line(position = position_dodge(width = 0.3)) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 16) +
  labs(y = "Indicator value", x = "Year") +
  scale_colour_viridis_d(option = "turbo", name = "Region")
Code 
summary_out |>
  unite(col = "CI_95", c(p_025, p_975), sep = " - ") |>
  unite(col = "IQR", c(p_25, p_75), sep = " - ") |>
  select(
    ECT,
    Indicator,
    ID,
    "Accounting area" = part,
    Ecosystem,
    "Closing year" = year,
    "Indicator value" = median,
    "95% CI" = CI_95,
    "50% CI" = IQR
  ) |>
  kable()

11. Export file

The indicator values are exported and can be found under data/results_NO_PTAR_001_v1.0.0.csv.

Export data 
write.csv(out, here::here("data/results_NO_PTAR_001_v1.0.0.csv"), row.names = F)

12. Author contributions

Not relevant, as this is the work of a single author. I would, however, like to acknowledge the work of those that created the indicator on Naturindeks (Nater and Eriksen 2025).

Session Info

This workflow uses renv. Run renv::restore() to recreate the working environment.
